name: Cannon Beach
features: Beach breaks near Haystack Rock, iconic Oregon coast

guide.hazards: Haystack Rock currents, sneaker waves, cold water
guide.crowd: Moderate to heavy in summer, light in winter
guide.description: Cannon Beach is one of Oregon's most iconic surf spots, sitting in the shadow of Haystack Rock. The beach break works on most swells and is forgiving enough for beginners while offering solid peaks for intermediates during bigger swells. The main break is south of Haystack Rock, with additional peaks stretching along the wide beach.

name: Indian Beach
features: Ecola State Park, consistent offshore peaks, sea stacks

guide.hazards: Rocky shoreline, strong currents, sea stacks, difficult access
guide.description: Indian Beach in Ecola State Park offers some of the most consistent surf on Oregon's north coast. Protected by the headland, it picks up swell from multiple directions. The rock-sand bottom creates defined peaks that are more powerful than neighboring beach breaks. The hike in keeps crowds manageable.

name: Short Sands
features: Oswald West State Park, horseshoe bay protected from NW winds

guide.bottom: Sandy beach break in a cove
guide.hazards: Rocks on south side, cold water, 15-min hike in
guide.crowd: Can get very crowded on good days
guide.description: Short Sands (Shorty's) at Oswald West State Park is arguably Oregon's most popular surf break. Nestled in a horseshoe-shaped cove, it's protected from prevailing NW winds and catches plenty of swell. The sandy bottom and mellow waves make it ideal for beginners and longboarders, though it can produce head-high+ waves during winter swells. The 15-minute hike through old-growth forest adds to the magic.

name: Manzanita
features: Beach break with scenic coastal town

guide.hazards: Strong currents, sneaker waves, cold water
guide.description: Manzanita offers a long stretch of exposed beach break that picks up plenty of swell. It's less protected than Short Sands, so wind is more of a factor, but when conditions align it can deliver fun, punchy peaks. The quaint town of Manzanita makes for a great surf trip base.

name: Oceanside
features: Tunnel to secret beach, varied breaks

guide.hazards: Rocky bottom, strong currents at tunnel beach
guide.description: Oceanside is a picturesque small village with a beach break that works best on mid-sized W-NW swells. The main beach faces west and picks up plenty of swell. A tunnel through the cliff leads to a more sheltered beach on the south side. The rock-sand bottom can produce wedgy waves.

name: Pacific City / Cape Kiwanda
features: Sandy-bottom breaks for beginners, point breaks for advanced

guide.hazards: Shore break, Cape Kiwanda cliffs, dory boats launching
guide.description: Pacific City at Cape Kiwanda is one of Oregon's most versatile surf spots. The beach break south of the cape is gentle and great for beginners, while the area closer to the sandstone headland produces steeper, more powerful waves. The dory fleet launches off the beach — give them right of way. The Pelican Pub sits right on the beach for post-surf refreshments.

name: Lincoln City
features: Long stretch of beach, Road's End popular

guide.hazards: Sneaker waves, strong currents, cold water
guide.description: Lincoln City's seven miles of beach offer numerous peaks to choose from. Road's End at the north end is the most popular surf spot, partially protected by the headland. The D River access in the middle of town and the beaches near Taft also produce quality waves. Lincoln City Surf Shop is the local hub for conditions info.

name: Nelscott Reef
features: Big wave spot, hosts competitions

guide.hazards: Extremely dangerous, tow-in only, hold-downs, cold water, rocks
guide.description: Nelscott Reef is Oregon's premier big wave spot, located about half a mile offshore from Lincoln City. It breaks on deep-water reef at 25-40+ feet and is strictly a tow-in affair. The reef was rediscovered in 2003 and has since hosted invitational big wave competitions. Only for experienced big wave surfers with proper safety teams.

name: Otter Rock
features: Partially protected from NW winds, consistent

guide.hazards: Rocky entry, cobblestones, cold water
guide.description: Otter Rock (Devil's Punchbowl) offers a partially protected cove that picks up swell consistently. The cobblestone-sand bottom creates fun peaks that are manageable for beginners on smaller days. The main break is in the cove south of Devil's Punchbowl. Access is easy from the parking lot.

name: South Beach / Newport
features: Gentle beach break, South Beach State Park

guide.hazards: Jetty currents, sneaker waves, cold water
guide.description: South Beach in Newport is a long, exposed beach break south of the Yaquina Bay jetty. The jetty provides some protection and can create sandbars that focus the swell. South Beach State Park offers easy access and parking. Newport's aquarium and bayfront are nearby for non-surfing family members.

name: Florence South Jetty
features: Siuslaw River meets Pacific, gentle break for longboards

guide.hazards: Jetty currents, river mouth currents, sneaker waves
guide.description: Florence South Jetty sits where the Siuslaw River meets the Pacific Ocean. The jetty creates sandbars that produce fun, mellow waves ideal for longboarding. It can handle bigger swells too, when the sandbar is right. The Oregon Dunes stretch south from here. Access is from South Jetty Road.

name: Coos Bay
features: Varied breaks along the bay

guide.hazards: Strong currents, rocky areas, cold water, isolated
guide.description: Coos Bay area offers several beach breaks along Bastendorff Beach and nearby shores. Bastendorff Beach, just west of the bay mouth, is the most accessible and consistent. The jetty at the bay entrance can create quality sandbars. This is uncrowded surf territory with a rugged, remote feel.

name: Bandon
features: Face Rock, scenic coastline

guide.hazards: Rocks, sea stacks, cold water, isolated
guide.description: Bandon's beach is one of Oregon's most scenic surf spots, with dramatic sea stacks including iconic Face Rock. The beach break between the rocks can produce quality waves, especially on S-SW swells that other north coast spots miss. The town of Bandon (Old Town) has a charming coastal vibe. Bullards Beach State Park is nearby.

name: Gold Beach
features: Rogue River mouth, southern Oregon

guide.bottom: Sandy beach break near river mouth
guide.hazards: River currents, sneaker waves, isolated, cold water
guide.description: Gold Beach sits at the mouth of the Rogue River in southern Oregon. The beach break north of the river mouth picks up both W and SW swells. The river mouth creates shifting sandbars that can produce quality peaks. This is remote, uncrowded surfing — you may have the lineup to yourself.

name: Brookings
features: Oregon's southernmost break, warmer water

guide.hazards: Rocky areas, strong currents, isolated
guide.description: Brookings is Oregon's southernmost surf spot, just north of the California border. The beach breaks around the Chetco River mouth offer the state's warmest water temperatures due to a unique microclimate. Harris Beach State Park has several accessible breaks. The banana belt climate makes this a relatively pleasant cold-water surf destination.

name: Rockaway Beach
features: Visitor Center area, family-friendly

guide.hazards: Sneaker waves, cold water
guide.description: Rockaway Beach is a family-friendly seven-mile stretch of sand on Oregon's north coast. The gentle beach break is perfect for beginners and longboarders. Multiple access points along the town make it easy to find a peak to yourself. The Twin Rocks offshore provide a scenic backdrop.

name: Atlantic Beach
features: The Circle, Oceanana Pier, closest break to Beaufort

guide.hazards: Shallow sandbars, strong longshore currents, pier pilings (stay 50+ yards away)
guide.description: Atlantic Beach is the Crystal Coast's most accessible surf spot, just across the bridge from Beaufort. The Circle area is the main hub with public parking and seasonal lifeguards. Oceanana Pier creates sandbars that focus the swell. South-facing exposure means it lights up on tropical swells and southwest wind events that miss the Outer Banks entirely.

name: Emerald Isle
features: Bogue Inlet Pier, long sandy beach, EILiveSurf cam

guide.bottom: Sandy beach break with shifting sandbars
guide.hazards: Shallow sandbars at low tide, pier pilings at Bogue Inlet Pier, jellyfish in summer
guide.description: Emerald Isle is a laid-back beach town on the western end of Bogue Banks. The south-facing beach picks up tropical swells well. Bogue Inlet Pier creates larger waves near its pilings (maintain distance). Sandbars shift frequently with storms, so conditions change week to week. Hot Wax Surf Shop and Bert's are the local hubs.

name: Onslow Beach
features: Camp Lejeune base beach, uncrowded

guide.hazards: Military base access restrictions, strong currents
guide.description: Onslow Beach sits on Camp Lejeune Marine Corps Base. Access is restricted to military personnel and authorized visitors, which keeps it extremely uncrowded. The south-facing beach picks up tropical swells and can produce fun, punchy beach break waves when conditions align.

name: Seaview Pier
features: North Topsail Beach, pier-adjacent peaks

guide.hazards: Pier pilings, rip currents near pier
guide.description: Seaview Pier on North Topsail Beach creates sandbars that concentrate swell energy. The peaks near the pier tend to be bigger and more defined than the open beach. Maintain safe distance from pilings. A good option when Topsail proper is too crowded.

name: Jolly Roger Pier
features: Topsail Island, consistent pier break

guide.hazards: Pier pilings, strong currents on bigger days, shallow inside
guide.description: Jolly Roger Pier (formerly Topsail Fishing Pier) is one of Topsail Island's best-known breaks. The pier creates a defined sandbar that produces steeper, more powerful waves than the surrounding open beach. Works on both tropical swells from the south and nor'easter energy from the northeast.

name: Surf City Pier
features: Heart of Topsail Island, active surf scene

guide.hazards: Pier pilings, crowds on good days, rip currents
guide.description: Surf City Pier sits at the heart of Topsail Island's surf scene. The pier focuses swell into defined peaks. This is where the local surf community congregates — expect company on good days. The town has a fun, low-key vibe with local shops and restaurants within walking distance.

name: Topsail Beach
features: Southern tip of Topsail Island, mellow beach break

guide.hazards: Inlet currents near south end, shallow sandbars
guide.description: Topsail Beach is the quieter southern end of Topsail Island. The mellow beach break is forgiving for beginners and longboarders. The inlet at the south end creates shifting sandbars that can produce fun wedges. Less crowded than the Surf City area.

name: North End Wrightsville
features: Shell Island area, less crowded than C Street

guide.hazards: Strong currents near inlet, shifting sandbars
guide.description: The north end of Wrightsville Beach near the Mason Inlet offers more room to spread out than the packed lineups further south. Shifting sandbars near the inlet can create quality peaks. Access via Shell Island Resort area.

name: C Street
features: Wrightsville Beach's most popular break, hollow barrels on bigger swells

guide.hazards: Crowds, shallow inside, strong rip currents on bigger days
guide.description: C Street is Wrightsville Beach's premier surf spot and the epicenter of the local surf scene. On larger swells it can produce hollow, barreling waves that rival anything on the southeast coast. The lineup is competitive — this is where NC's best surfers congregate. Multiple surf shops, restaurants, and the iconic Wrightsville bar scene are steps away.

name: Blockade Runner
features: Near Blockade Runner hotel, mellow peaks

guide.hazards: Swimmers in summer, rip currents
guide.description: The stretch near the Blockade Runner hotel offers mellower peaks than C Street, making it a good option for intermediate surfers or when the main breaks are too crowded. The wider beach and gentler slope produce softer waves that are forgiving on smaller days.

name: South End Wrightsville
features: Masonboro Inlet adjacent, powerful on bigger swells

guide.hazards: Strong inlet currents, shifting sandbars, sharks near inlet
guide.description: South End Wrightsville near Masonboro Inlet can produce the most powerful waves on the island when conditions align. The inlet bathymetry focuses swell energy and creates steeper, faster waves. Respect the inlet currents — they are strong and can sweep you out quickly.

name: Ocracoke
features: Remote barrier island, accessible via ferry from Beaufort/Cedar Island

guide.hazards: Isolation (limited services), strong currents, no lifeguards
guide.description: Ocracoke Island is accessible from Beaufort via the 2.25-hour Cedar Island ferry. The uncrowded beach breaks stretch for miles with nobody around. Waves are less consistent than Hatteras to the north but when a swell hits, the empty peaks make it worthwhile. Reservations for the ferry are strongly recommended.

name: Hatteras Ferry Docks
features: Southern tip of Hatteras Island, near Ocracoke ferry

guide.hazards: Strong inlet currents, boat traffic near ferry channel
guide.description: The beach near the Hatteras-Ocracoke ferry docks can produce quality waves when northeast swells wrap into the area. The inlet bathymetry creates interesting sandbars. Less crowded than the more famous breaks to the north.

name: Cape Hatteras Lighthouse
features: East Coast's most iconic break, barreling lefts, First Jetty

guide.bestSeason: Sep-Mar (peak: fall hurricanes and winter nor'easters)
guide.hazards: Powerful hollow waves, strong currents, shallow sandbars, localism
guide.crowd: Heavy on good days — this is the East Coast's premier break
guide.description: Cape Hatteras Lighthouse (First Jetty/The Point) is where East Coast professional surfing was born. The narrow continental shelf allows swells to hit relatively unfiltered, producing hollow, powerful barrels that rival anything on the Atlantic seaboard. Fall hurricanes and winter nor'easters deliver the goods. Expect crowds and respect the locals.

name: North Buxton
features: Stone jetties, quality sandbars north of Cape Point

guide.hazards: Jetty rocks, strong currents, powerful shore break
guide.description: North Buxton features stone jetties that create quality sandbars, producing consistent and well-shaped waves. Less intense than the Lighthouse but still delivers solid surf on northeast and east swells. The jetties provide a useful reference point for positioning in the lineup.

name: Frisco
features: South-facing beach, lights up on summer south swells

guide.hazards: Strong currents, remote location
guide.description: Frisco's south-facing beach angle gives it an advantage over other OBX spots during summer. When southwest winds push and south swells arrive from tropical systems, Frisco can be pumping while the rest of Hatteras is flat. Also works well on east and northeast swells in fall and winter.

name: Avon Pier
features: Classic OBX pier break, consistent sandbars

guide.hazards: Pier pilings, strong rip currents, hollow shore break
guide.description: Avon Pier is one of the more consistent breaks on Hatteras Island. The pier creates defined sandbars that focus swell energy into quality peaks. The east-facing beach catches both north and south swells. Avon village has a small but dedicated surf community.

name: Rodanthe Pier
features: Powerful beach break, long rides, fewer crowds than S-Turns

guide.hazards: Powerful shore break, pier pilings, strong currents
guide.description: Rodanthe Pier sits between the lighthouse to the south and Oregon Inlet to the north. The powerful beach break produces long, fast rides. Less crowded than nearby S-Turns, making it a good option when the more popular spots are packed. The narrow island width means waves can break close to shore.

name: S-Turns
features: End of Pea Island, Hatteras Island's most popular break

guide.bottom: Sandy beach break with shifting sandbars
guide.hazards: Powerful hollow waves, strong rip currents, crowds, shifting sandbars
guide.crowd: Heavy — the most popular break on Hatteras Island
guide.description: S-Turns at the end of Pea Island is currently the most popular break on Hatteras Island. Large swells from nor'easters and hurricanes produce powerful, hollow waves that attract surfers from across the East Coast. Named for the S-shaped curves in the road. Expect crowds and a competitive lineup on good days.
